posts.name: Brian S t o t t e r
posts.cooked:
• 2023 Henri Magnien Gevrey-Chambertin Vieilles Vignes - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Gevrey-Chambertin (4/30/2025)
    Splash decanted and poured in a Grassl Cru, followed over the afternoon. So much going on in the nose, rich and expressive but not overly ripe. Dried blackberry, blueberry, black raspberry, purple flowers, a little meat/game bird, white smoke, and cocoa powder on the nose, shifting in focus between black and red fruit with each swirl of the glass. Fresh and juicy with mouthwatering acidity, on the attack it feels like there’s going to be a big tannic grip but instead it gently blankets the palate leaving a long finish of lingering dried black fruits and some subtle herbal and fresh soil tones. Absolutely loved this wine and don’t think my remaining 3 bottles will be enough.

    Posted from CellarTracker

    IMG_32553024×4032 2.59 MB
posts.display_username: Brian S t o t t e r

posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked:
When I see Magnien on the label, I typically run for the hills.

But Charles is a rising star. The wines are juicy and fresh. They have some excellent plots in quality vineyards too. These are plush wines that provide good value.

Vins Rare has been early champion and has nice selection.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: I think henri magnien is definitely one to check out, especially the Ruchottes! For those in south Florida mr d has the 22 for 275.

posts.name: Brian S t o t t e r
posts.cooked: There’s Henri and Michel Magnien making wine in Burgundy from what I can tell. What turned you off from the one(s) you had?
posts.display_username: Brian S t o t t e r

posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked: Back in the day, Frederic Magnien and Michel Magnien made oaky, clumsy wines IMO. Nothing awful but not a lot of freshness or grace …

reply_to_user.name: Brian S t o t t e r

posts.name: Brian S t o t t e r
posts.cooked: Not sure how long ago that was but I did not get that impression at all with this bottling. Oak use was judicious and this had a vibrancy and freshness I’ve struggled to find with other “under the radar” Burg producers I’ve tried lately.
posts.display_username: Brian S t o t t e r

posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked:
Yes Brian. That’s my point.

Charles is part of the Next Gen doing great things at traditional houses. Less extraction and more meticulous farming.

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked: I really like what Henri is doing and have been buying for a few years. Perhaps Cazetiers is sweet spot for me but they’re all quite good.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Historically, they have had very good Pinot genetics (like the Dugats, one of their ancestors worked in an important local nursery and selected good lineages, to the extent that locally some talk of “Pinot Magnien”). And indeed very good plots, including the only parcel of Cazetiers that runs from the top to the bottom of the climat. The famous 1955 Leroy Cazetiers came from them.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
Chsrles is going experimental Cazetiers wild with his 2023s, ageing in various vessels for various periods of time to sse what potential they bring. It will be cool to compare the first bottling with the various cuvée to be bottled later.
I import these, which us where Stotter"s bottle came from.
I got the 2023s here before the tariffs.

posts.name: Brian S t o t t e r
posts.cooked: I opened the G-C 1er Cru Les Cazetiers last night, no detailed notes but at least on the first night I had a strong preference for the VV. Quality winemaking, the Cazetiers had a nice precision to it but the strawberry/cherry fruit flavors were a little too candied for my preference and I got less depth of meaty/minerality notes I loved in the VV. Will see what the rest of my bottle brings tonight.
